story:
Snow fell softly as Alex stood outside her cabin, the pines wearing white like furred coats. She held her husband’s urn, turned her back to the wind, and tipped it, refusing to let the ashes touch her hands. The bruises on her skin had long faded, but the ones beneath would not. Still, this felt like a beginning—letting him drift away into the cold night.

Under Jacob O’Neal’s rule she had learned hard lessons: not to trust her heart, her body, or her soul to anyone. He had beaten those rules into her until silence felt safer than breath. She knew they weren’t truths, not really, but they clung like scars. For now, her goal was simple. Put him down into the snow-dark air and clear him out of her life.

The cabin had once been a refuge. She’d bought it ten years ago with money from quilts and paintings, and she slipped away only when Jacob was out of town—weekends, a few stolen days—far from pitying looks and the lies about falling down stairs. Back home, everyone knew even if they didn’t say it outright. Here she could rest.

Five years ago, he found the place. He arrived on a Monday evening, flinging the door open so hard it slammed the wall. He called her by the full name he used when he wanted her to shake, accused her of using his money, jerked her to her feet, and promised she’d pay. She said nothing about the truth—that the cabin was hers, bought with her art—because truth only added fuel. He never hit her face. He never broke bones. He knew exactly what he was doing. When he finished, he stood in the doorway like a landlord and told her it was his cabin now. She didn’t come back after that. He did. The rods and mounts and trophies arrived with him, a life he hung on her walls.

Last week, the hospital called. A gentle voice asked if she was Mrs. O’Neal and if she could come in, preferably with someone to drive her. Before she could answer, the voice added, “I know this is a relief for you—you will grab the counter so you don’t fall.” She understood at once. Jacob had been drinking, celebrating another big sale. He bullied people for a living and called it success. On the way to buy himself something shiny, he missed a turn, jumped the guardrail, and his truck plunged into the river. He drowned before help could reach him. Relief hit her so hard she had to hold the counter to keep from sinking, and then she folded herself into the careful performance of a widow.

Now, inside, the fire crackled and the snow hushed the forest. She pulled a blanket tight and looked around at what her refuge had become: fishing poles stacked in a neat line; mounted antlers arranged with practiced pride; dull wood where he’d insisted on varnish she never wanted. It wasn’t hers anymore. He had taken even this.

Tomorrow she would drive back to town for the service, for the hands and the murmured condolences, for the nods at a grief they mistook for love. Afterward, she would come here once more, pack what still belonged to her, and disappear. No children tied her. He hadn’t let her keep friends. Her parents were gone. She would leave only the past behind, and even that she hoped the weather would soften—like snow smoothing the edges of a dark world—until she could step forward without looking back.
